summary: Gruber examines the fundamental use-case problem facing Apple's rumored VR/XR headset, building on Matthew Ball's essay about why VR/AR keeps falling short of predictions. He systematically works through every potential use case — gaming, video, virtual meetings, and projected displays — and finds each one lacking a compelling justification for a $1,000+ device. He draws a historical parallel between AR glasses and Alan Kay's 1972 Dynabook concept, arguing that true all-day AR glasses are likely 15 years away from the first great VR goggles. While he isn't dismissing Apple's headset outright, he genuinely struggles to imagine what would make consumers want to buy, carry, and use it alongside their existing devices.
key_points: Every major VR/AR prediction from tech leaders (Zuckerberg, Sweeney) has missed its timeline by years, and practical all-day AR glasses remain far off

There's a consistent historical pattern where Apple enters a category about 5 years after clumsy first-generation products appear, shipping the paradigm-defining version — they're never first because they don't ship prototypes
Each potential headset use case (gaming, video, meetings, virtual displays) has a fundamental flaw that undermines the value proposition at a $1,000+ price point
Every successful Apple platform has been fundamentally driven by fun, raising the question of what makes a VR headset fun enough to justify its cost and inconvenience
A 4K-per-eye headset cannot replicate the pixel density and field-of-view quality of Apple's own 5K Studio Display, undermining the virtual display use case
money_quotes: This is, quite literally, the billions-dollar question: What are the intended use cases for Apple's headset?
It's not that Apple is seldom first — they're never first, because they don't ship their prototypes.
Every successful platform Apple has ever established has been fundamentally driven by fun.
The 'killer app' was that it already had dozens of them.
I am convinced we will get there. I am equally convinced we are not close to being able to make such devices.
A virtual meeting where everyone else is participating Zoom-style but you are wearing a VR headset is going to make you look weird.

I cannot believe that such a headset would be intended for wearing around as you go about daily life, augmenting the real world with virtual displays and ambient contextual information. That's what we need AR glasses for, not VR goggles.
Do you think Apple is going to ship a virtual reality keyboard where you move your fingers in the air that works as well as even an iPad touchscreen keyboard? I do not.
key_insight: Despite great anticipation for Apple's VR headset, every plausible use case falls short of justifying a $1,000+ device that must be carried alongside the phones, laptops, and tablets people already own — yet Apple must believe it has answers, or it wouldn't bring it to market.
